q: Which of the following is a detectable change in the internal or external environment of an organism?
Biological growth is generally characterized as a permanent and _______ increase in size.
The primary source of metabolic energy required for plant growth and development is derived from _______.
Plant development involves both the formation of new structures and the specialization of cells, known as cellular _______.
Plant growth regulators synthesized internally by plant tissues are known as _______.
Phytohormones can be broadly categorized into two major operational groups: growth promoters and growth _______.
The first group of plant hormones discovered, which primarily promote cell elongation, are the _______.
The production of seedless fruits without prior fertilization of unpollinated ovaries is called _______.
Which plant hormone is unique due to its structural identification as a simple purine derivative?
Which of the following phytohormones is chemically unique because it exists as a gas at room temperature?
The gaseous plant hormone ethylene plays a major physiological role by promoting fruit _______.
During autumn, the breakdown of green chlorophyll pigments unmasks yellow and orange accessory pigments called _______.
Abscisic acid chemically functions to induce and maintain seed _______ under unfavorable conditions.
During seed germination, the embryonic root structure that first breaks through the micropyle is the _______.
A developmental response where a plant's physiological processes are regulated by the relative length of day and night is called _______.
The hypothetical, leaf-derived flowering hormone that triggers floral bud development is known as _______.
The specialized, light-sensitive protein pigment that regulates photomorphogenesis in plants is _______.
The biological phenomenon where seeds germinate while still physically attached to the parent plant is known as _______.
Which major organ system in animals coordinates rapid responses by transmitting electrical impulses?
The structural division of the nervous system consisting exclusively of the brain and the spinal cord is the _______.
The major brain region that serves as the primary integration center for voluntary muscle movements, logic, and speech is the _______.
The cerebral cortex contains localized areas specialized for processing incoming signals from the eyes and ears, known as _______ areas.
The olfactory bulbs are specialized, anterior extensions of the forebrain designed to process the sense of _______.
The brain stem area that plays a major role in bridge-like signaling between the cerebellum and the cerebrum is the _______.
The delicate membranes that wrap around and provide physical protection to the brain and spinal cord are called _______.
The human body contains a total of thirty-one pairs of peripheral nerves that emerge directly from the _______.
The division of the peripheral nervous system that carries voluntary commands to skeletal muscle effectors is the _______ system.
The autonomic nervous system is structurally and functionally subdivided into the sympathetic and _______ divisions.
Under stressful, high-alert situations, physiological changes such as increased heart rate are driven by the _______ nervous system.
Activation of the parasympathetic nervous system promotes energy conservation, characterized by an increase in _______ activity.
The basic functional and structural communication cells of the animal nervous system are called _______.
The non-neuronal supporting cells within the nervous tissue that protect and nourish neurons are collectively called _______.
The long, unbranched neuronal process that conducts action potentials away from the cell body toward a target is the _______.
The specialized gaps between adjacent myelin segments where the axonal membrane is exposed are called nodes of _______.
Neurons that form complex interconnecting pathways exclusively within the central nervous system are classified as _______.
The structures that execute physiological responses to motor commands, such as muscles and glands, are called _______.
The functional microscopic gap across which a nerve impulse is chemically transmitted between two cells is a _______.
During neurotransmitter release, synaptic vesicles fuse with the presynaptic membrane via a transport mechanism called _______.
The principal neurotransmitter responsible for stimulating skeletal muscle fiber contraction at the neuromuscular junction is _______.
Parkinson's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder fundamentally caused by an inadequate supply of the neurotransmitter _______.
Clinical depression is frequently treated using medications designed to correct a functional deficiency of _______.
Sensory receptors act as transducers, converting diverse environmental energy forms into _______ signals.
The tough, white, fibrous outer structural coat that maintains the spherical shape of the human eyeball is the _______.
The clear, transparent anterior modification of the sclera that allows light rays to pass into the eyeball is the _______.
The heavily pigmented middle layer of the human eye that functions to absorb stray light and prevent internal glare is the _______.
The colored, circular muscular diaphragm that controls the amount of light entering the inner chambers of the eye is the _______.
The innermost neural layer of the human eye that contains the specialized light-sensitive photoreceptor cells is the _______.
The specific photoreceptor cells in the human retina that are highly sensitive to low-intensity light and enable night vision are _______.
The clear, watery fluid filling the anterior and posterior chambers situated between the cornea and the lens is the _______.
The large, gelatinous mass that fills the major posterior cavity behind the crystalline lens to maintain eyeball shape is the _______.
The cranial nerve that leaves the eyeball to transmit visual electrical signals to the occipital lobe of the brain is the _______ nerve.
The point on the retina where the optic nerve exits the eyeball, completely lacking photoreceptors, is known as the _______.
The master endocrine gland closely linked to the hypothalamus at the base of the brain is the _______ gland.
The fundamental biological process of maintaining a dynamic, stable internal environment within an organism is called _______.
Hormones are transported from their ductless endocrine glands of origin to their distant target cells via the _______.
The primary pancreatic hormone secreted by beta cells to facilitate glucose uptake and lower blood sugar levels is _______.
The pancreatic hormone secreted by alpha cells that triggers the breakdown of glycogen to elevate blood glucose levels is _______.
The major endocrine glands located on top of the kidneys that secrete aldosterone and adrenaline are the _______ glands.
The primary male sex hormone produced by the testes that regulates sperm cell production is _______.
The active physiological transport of sodium ions across renal tubules is regulated by a steroid hormone called _______.
A rapid, stereotyped, and automatic involuntary response to an environmental stimulus is defined as a _______.
Which type of neuron is responsible for conducting incoming sensory impulses toward the central nervous system?
The specialized supportive neuroglial cells that produce the insulating myelin sheath around peripheral axons are _______ cells.
Opiate drugs mimic natural endorphins and function clinically to manage severe pain as potent _______.
The core physiological mechanism by which the lens of the human eye modifies its focal thickness is called _______.
The specialized neuroglia responsible for synthesizing myelin sheaths within the central nervous system are _______.
The primary metabolic rate of the human body is chemically regulated by a hormone called _______.
The protective fluid that circulates within the ventricles of the brain and the central canal of the spinal cord is _______.
The somatic nervous system primarily regulates the voluntary contractions of _______ muscles.
The rapid, leaping mode of electrical impulse propagation along a myelinated axon is called _______ conduction.
The involuntary closing of the eyelids in response to a sudden flash of light or incoming object is a _______ reflex.
Which hormone induces strong rhythmic contractions of uterine smooth muscles during childbirth?
The primary endocrine organs responsible for producing estrogen and progesterone in females are the _______.
The brain region that acts as the principal coordination center for complex motor skills, balance, and posture is the _______.
The major brain structure that acts as a vital bridge between the nervous system and the endocrine system is the _______.
The structural site where a motor neuron axon terminal closely approaches a skeletal muscle fiber membrane is the _______ junction.
The neural pathway that physically and functionally connects the left and right cerebral hemispheres is the corpus _______.
Phagocytic glial cells that clear cellular debris, waste products, and pathogens from the central nervous system are _______.
The brief reversal of membrane electrical potential that propagates self-regeneratively along an excited axon is an _______.
The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is actively maintained by the sodium-_______ exchange pump.
Chemical messengers that regulate long-term biological processes like growth, development, and metabolism are called _______.
Hormones derived from lipids containing a characteristic four-ring carbon skeleton are classified as _______ hormones.
The hormone that directly increases water reabsorption in the collecting ducts of the kidneys is _______.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine initiate the physiological responses necessary for a fight-or-_______ reaction.
The protective gastrointestinal reflex that forcefully expels toxic substances from the stomach is _______.
The neurochemical pathway associated with mood regulation, sleep cycles, and emotional balance relies heavily on _______.
Chronic down-regulation of post-synaptic receptors due to drug abuse often leads to psychological and physical _______.
The curved surface of the cornea performs the primary role of light bending as it _______ incoming rays.
The specialized photoreceptor cells adapted for high-acuity color vision in bright light are _______ cells.
In bright light, the circular smooth muscles of the iris contract to cause pupil _______.
The ciliary body smooth muscles relax when the eye focuses on extremely _______ objects.
The endocrine disorder characterized by an inability of cells to uptake glucose due to insulin deficiency is diabetes _______.
The protective skeletal structure that completely encloses and shields the human brain is the _______.
Sensory receptors located within muscles and joints that monitor body position and movement are _______.
The medulla oblongata functions as a crucial vital center regulating autonomic cardiorespiratory _______.
The central H-shaped region of the spinal cord is composed of unmyelinated structures called _______ matter.
Severe mechanical injury or transection of the spinal cord can result in permanent body _______.
The peripheral nerves that specifically emerge from the base of the brain are called _______ nerves.
The human body possesses twelve pairs of cranial nerves and thirty-one pairs of _______ nerves.
An action potential causes the opening of voltage-gated channels, allowing an influx of _______ ions into the terminal.
The progressive loss of memory and cognitive function in elderly individuals is characteristic of _______ disease.
The brain region that acts as a sensory relay station, filtering and directing data to the cerebrum, is the _______.
The specialized capillary networks that produce cerebrospinal fluid within the brain ventricles are the _______ plexuses.
The main component of the protective blood-brain barrier is formed by endothelial cells and supporting _______.
A drug that mimics or enhances the normal physiological activity of a neurotransmitter is an _______.
A chemical substance that blocks the action of a neurotransmitter at a synapse is called an _______.
The individual cellular units that form the functional communication network of the nervous system are _______.
Nerve fibers that are wrapped in a protective lipid-rich myelin sheath are classified as _______ fibers.
The spinal cord is physically protected from mechanical shocks by the surrounding bony _______.
The short, highly branched cytoplasmic extensions that conduct impulses toward a neuron cell body are _______.
The micro-gap separating the presynaptic membrane from the postsynaptic membrane is the synaptic _______.
The tiny membrane-bound spheres that store neurotransmitter molecules inside the axon terminal are synaptic _______.
Stereoscopic vision provides humans with a complex, three-_______ perception of their environment.
The mechanism by which the eye adjusts its lens thickness to focus on near or distant objects is called _______.
The two muscle groups of the iris operate in an _______ manner to control light entry.
The crystalline structure of the human eye is shape-classified as a _______ lens.
When entering a dimly lit room, the radial muscles contract to cause pupil _______.
Progesterone prepares the inner lining of the uterus for pregnancy and stimulates growth of the _______ glands.
The specialized neuron that transmits electrical impulses entirely within the central nervous system is an _______.
Activation of the parasympathetic division stimulates the flow of digestive secretions from the _______ glands.
The sympathetic division increases heart rate and causes relaxation of the bronchioles within the _______.
The sympathetic system slows down the motility and digestive activity of the stomach and _______ walls.
The contraction of the internal sphincter to inhibit micturition is promoted by _______ stimulation.
The urinary _______ walls relax while its sphincter contracts under sympathetic dominance.
The specialized organelles that provide the ATP required for active ion transport in a neuron are _______.
The lipid-rich insulating substance that wraps around many peripheral nerve fibers is called _______.
The precise physical response of a muscle fiber to an action potential at a neuromuscular junction is _______.
The white outer layer of the eye is structurally composed of dense _______ connective tissue.
The central aperture in the center of the iris through which light enters the inner eye is the _______.
The cranial nerve fibers that leave the eyeball converge at a point on the retina called the _______ disk.
The iris muscles arranged like the spokes of a wheel are called the _______ muscles.
A hormone that directly stimulates the elongation of skeletal bones and growth of tissues is _______ hormone.
The hormone that causes the thyroid gland to synthesize and release its metabolic hormones is _______.
A specialized cell structure that detects a specific type of environmental change is a _______.
The brain region that acts as the principal coordination center for complex motor skills and posture is the _______.
The neuron that releases neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft is called the _______ neuron.
The membrane of the target cell that contains receptors for neurotransmitters is the _______ membrane.
The part of the central nervous system that runs continuously down through the vertebral column is the _______.
The single long cytoplasmic process that transmits action potentials away from the neuronal soma is an _______.
The structural site where an axon terminal closely approaches an adjacent cell membrane is a _______.
Neurons that process and integrate incoming sensory data before sending motor commands are called _______.
The size of the pupil is adjusted by the actions of circular and _______ muscles in the iris.
The watery fluid that provides nutrients to the non-vascular lens and cornea is the _______ humor.
The round shape and internal hydrostatic pressure of the anterior eye chamber are maintained by the _______ humor.
The iris muscles that run circumferentially and shrink the pupil aperture are called _______ muscles.
Chemical substances synthesized in minute amounts by ductless glands and transported by blood are _______.
The hormone secreted by the adrenal cortex that regulates sodium and water retention is _______.
A hormone produced by the ovaries that prepares the uterine lining for the implantation of an embryo is _______.
Endocrine glands empty their secretions directly into the surrounding tissue fluid and capillary _______.
The major control system that achieves coordination via rapid electrical nerve impulses is the _______ system.
The structural division of the nervous system that includes all cranial and spinal nerves is the _______ nervous system.
The folded outer layer of gray matter covering the cerebral hemispheres is the cerebral _______.
The midbrain contains important reflex centers for coordinating the movement of the head and the _______.
The division of the peripheral nervous system that regulates internal visceral organs involuntarily is the _______ system.
The sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions cooperate to maintain visceral internal _______.
The swollen structures at the end of axon branches that store neurotransmitters are axon _______.
The tiny fluid-filled gap that separates two communicating neurons at a synapse is the synaptic _______.
